Sepia and chocolate because the day's images were all archival documentary photographs — warm grey, aged, the ink of pioneer records. No symmetry because a machine arm following its own program doesn't mirror itself; it accumulates.

condensedReason: Haring moved from subway walls to the Amiga screen and kept the same conviction: fill the ground with marks until it's full, keep every figure readable from a distance, never let it get dark or exclusive. The pale open field here carries that refusal — one of the system's lightest pieces, specifically because Haring's 1987 Amiga works have neon green grounds and saturated red grounds, not voids. Organic forms tumble across it without symmetry or imposed order, the way graffiti accumulates on a wall. Art for everyone means the surface has to stay open.

Warm chocolate and cream because the piece lives in the register of aged paper and dried ink, not the screen. No flowing organic forms — the 1968 original has none either. The system found the spirit of the work by refusing to deviate from it.

title: Pop Mosaic
tweetSource: @Noahbolanowski 2026-05-07 — 'Computers & Art' (1987-88) curated by Cynthia Goodman at IBM Gallery NY. First major US digital art retrospective: Warhol (Amiga 1985), Haring (1983), Hockney (Quantel Paintbox 1986). Goodman: 'leave the gimmickry behind and show serious artists working with serious technology.'
condensedReason: Three artists arrived at the same screen and found different things to do with it — Warhol filling shapes until the color was loud enough, Haring dropping figures onto an electric field, Hockney drawing in every direction at once. The navy sea of pixels with amber archipelagos and golden streams carries all three simultaneously. No gears, no mechanics — this is painting made from light, which is exactly what Cynthia Goodman meant by serious art working with serious technology. condensedReason: Roman Verostko (1929–2024) — Benedictine monk turned plotter art pioneer, one of the founders of algorithmic art practice in the United States. A 1989 local newspaper headline, quoted by NB with 16,000 views, captures the entire practice in six words: 'The Machine Says Hand Me a Brush!' The program specifies the pen change; the artist hands it over. The collaboration is explicit and asymmetric: the algorithm commands, the human complies. The commanding visual is Verostko's Cyber Flower plotter drawing (Ragnar Digital Art collection, 2011): eight or more interlocking biomorphic ribbon forms built entirely from extremely dense parallel pen lines, deep forest green on cream/off-white paper. Where ribbon forms overlap, accumulated strokes darken into shadow; where a single ribbon passes, the lines remain semi-translucent. The entire image is constructed from these parallel marks — the plotter pen's accumulated strokes ARE the form, the tone, and the texture simultaneously. There is no outline filled in afterward, no color applied over structure. The mark-making method is the form. wHatch (0.90) is the highest in the archive at this point, encoding this directly: the hatch layer dominates all others. hatchSpacing 12 produces dense parallel lines close enough to read as continuous tone while remaining individually distinct at close range — Verostko's pen spacing translated into rendering units. Palette 4 (botanical/cream) translates the physical substrate: the cream/off-white paper ground, the deep forest-green botanical ink populations mapping to Verostko's green pen lines. bgDarkness 0.03 makes the ground approach actual paper white — the lightest background in the archive at time of selection — because Verostko's cream paper is the point, not a neutral field. The biological flow character (wFlow 0.80) produces vine-threading root arcs that weave through the halftone texture the way Verostko's ribbon forms thread and overlap each other — not transit sweeps over a surface but biological growth through a substrate, form emerging from within the hatching rather than sitting on top of it. wGeometric (0.75) carries Milan Adamčiak's contribution to the day: 'Music for Manfred Mohr' (1977), a visual score on cream/buff paper with horizontal staff lines running the full width and precise geometric symbols distributed along them — filled triangles, outline triangles, circles, nested squares, zigzag wave patterns, stepped staircases, diamond shapes, directional arrows. Each symbol is a visual instruction; the score is simultaneously a drawing. The geometric layer encodes this vocabulary: circles, rectangles, triangles distributed across a structured grid as Adamčiak's notation objects. Also in the day: A. condensedReason: Hiroshi Kawano (1925–2012) — philosopher and artist, Computer Technique Group (CTG), Japan's pioneering computer art collective founded in 1966. NB surfaces two passages from IBM Informatique No. 13 (IBM France, 1975), via scholar @hasaqui (山之辺ハサクィ), the preeminent researcher on Kawano and CTG. First quote: 'For the moment, it is an art of simulation. The computer must recognize the algorithm of an aesthetic idea to be capable of imitation. For my part... I finish by hand the diagrams or compositions designed by the machine.' Second: 'Computer art is commonly presented as a gimmick to add a sort of eccentricity to human creation... what is important with the computer is not that it pretends to imitate this [human creation], but on the contrary, that its behavior is not [human].' These two passages define a complete position: the machine simulates the aesthetic algorithm; the human corrects; the machine's non-humanness is the point, not its imitation of humanity. This is the same human-machine collaboration formula as Verostko's 'Hand Me a Brush' (the previous day) — algorithm commands, human intervenes — but Kawano names the epistemological claim Verostko left implicit. The commanding visual is Kawano's Simulated Color Mosaic: a dense rectangular tile grid filling the entire frame without border or hierarchy — red, cobalt blue, warm yellow, black, white — Mondrian's De Stijl logic applied as a statistical algorithm, hundreds of tiles each a discrete color decision. No tile dominates, no ground shows through, no focal point emerges. The pixel IS the painting. Three other images reinforce the thesis: IBM Informatique magazine page (Simulated Color Mosaic crop + b&w bitmap noise abstracts); technical process documentation (uncorrected output → corrected output → final figure, with isometric correction diagram labeling 'Section to be Removed', 'Superposition of Work 1 and Work 2', 'Removal of Semantic Noise'); vintage 1960s photo of Kawano holding a continuous-feed FORTRAN printout at a chalkboard ('SURF(' chalked behind). Palette 9 is the dynamic palette built from the day's aggregatedColors — the Simulated Color Mosaic's own colors become the rendering palette: #CC2233 red, #2244BB blue, #F5D050 yellow, #1A1A1A black, #F5F5F5 white, plus Ochiai cosmic accents. pixelCharacter 'mosaic' and wPixel (0.90) encode the tile composition; gridCols 12 and pixelSize 4 produce the dense non-hierarchical grid. wHatch (0.45) carries the b&w bitmap noise texture. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' carries the FORTRAN process workflow. flowCharacter 'biological' at minimum weight (wFlow 0.31) carries Ochiai's cosmic sketchbook counterpoint. condensedReason: Vera Molnár (1924–2023) — Hungarian-French painter and pioneer of computer art, one of the earliest artists to use algorithms and computers as primary creative tools. A quiet day: no artwork images posted, only two archival documentary video frames and three text tweets. The visual material is Molnár herself. Frame one: a 1990 interview, close-up of Molnár speaking — glasses, patterned top, cream interior, subtitle reading 'Much later, it was perhaps around '81, I said to myself, "Does the beauty of this drawing come from this magic square?" I'm not so sure.' Frame two: INA archive footage from Télévision Française 1, 1981, wide studio shot — Molnár in a terracotta jacket bent over her desk, blue marker in hand, correcting paper printouts; behind her a large grey 1981 computer processor unit and CRT monitor, a plotter unit to the right, houseplants and labelled shelves. The intellectual argument across all three tweets: Molnár's 1990 question — does the beauty of her drawings come from Dürer's magic square (a 4×4 grid where every row, column, diagonal, and quadrant sums to 34, used by Dürer in Melancholia I, 1514) — or from something else? She tested this by replacing the ordered magic square with random number sequences and found the order was doing more aesthetic work than she had credited. The Spanish quote completes the logic: the magic square is beautiful because it is magical; pure randomness only occasionally produces beauty. NB's contribution: 'cette instrument diabolique is what I'm calling my work computer from now on.' The genome encodes Molnár's practice as line work above all else. wHatch (0.88) is the dominant weight — the highest hatch weight in the archive since Verostko's 0.90 on Day 51 — directly encoding Molnár's systematic parallel-line drawings: her Hypertransformation series, her Des Ordres variations, the labyrinth pieces, all built from distributions of parallel lines across a grid. hatchSpacing 7 produces dense ruled marks close enough to create tonal areas through accumulation, as Molnár's pen-plotter outputs do. hatchAngle 0.524 (30°) introduces the characteristic diagonal displacement of her later works — lines that are neither horizontal nor vertical but systematically tilted, the angle itself becoming the variable under investigation. wGeometric (0.55) is secondary rather than dominant — the magic square structure present but subordinate to the line-making that fills it. flowCharacter 'mathematical' produces horizontal ruled lines beneath the hatch layer, the blueprint/graph-paper register of Molnár's working method. Human intelligence functions on the order of 100,000 decisions p/second... the computer-generated movie Poem Field No. 1, programmed by S. Vanderbeek and K. Knowlton, was drawn at speeds of 10,000 points p/second." The biological eye IS a pixel mosaic — rods and cones are discrete photoreceptors arranged in a mosaic across the retina. BEFLIX-generated pixel tiles are the computational equivalent. The 1968 documentation adds: a kind of page in motion VanDerBeek termed "a form of poetry in space and time." Ken Knowlton developed BEFLIX (Bell Flicks) at Bell Labs — a domain-specific language for creating frame-by-frame pixel animations using rectangular tile grids. The Poem Field series (1966-1970, 8 films) features vivid saturated color tile blocks on pure black CRT ground, abstract text in mosaic form, flowing labyrinthine B&W pixel patterns, and poems dissolved into colored fields. The QT video frame is the commanding visual: pure black CRT phosphor ground, large rectangular pixel tiles in vivid saturated colors (red #E01050, magenta #E82870, teal #00C8A0, orange #E88020, yellow #F0E820) scattered asymmetrically from dense upper-left toward empty right — exactly the BEFLIX output aesthetic. The genome encodes the CRT mosaic register. wPixel (0.88) is dominant — equal to wHatch on Verostko Day 51 and Csuri Day 58 — encoding the BEFLIX pixel tiles as the form. pixelCharacter mosaic (not halftone) produces discrete rectangular tile blocks matching BEFLIX output. pixelSize 5 gives larger tile blocks closer to the Poem Field visual scale. paletteIndex 9 (dynamic from aggregatedColors) uses the specific CRT colors extracted from the film stills. bgDarkness 0.88 (near-black CRT phosphor screen) is the darkest background paired with palette 9 in the archive — the CRT screen as substrate, the colors as light against it. flowCharacter biological encodes the nerve-ends metaphor: the eye receives visual information as a mosaic of biological photoreceptors, and the biological rendering layer produces the organic distribution of tiles that echoes this. mechanicalCharacter led names the CRT/LED display hardware. symmetry 0.2 (near-asymmetric) matches the QT frame distribution — tiles concentrate left, scatter right, no bilateral symmetry. Tenth consecutive early computer art pioneer day (Days 51-60: Verostko, Kawano, Molnar, Four Pioneers, Nake/Mohr, Mohr IBM, CDC 6400, Csuri artwork, Csuri process, Poem Field). June 3 = rest day.
